title: Ādeśa to Perform Iriyāvahiyaṁ
summary: Standing before the sthāpanācārya after guru-vandana, hands joined in jina-mudrā and yoga-mudrā, the devotee asks the guru's leave (ādeśa) to make the Iriyāvahiyaṁ confession for harm done to living beings while moving; the guru grants it, the devotee accepts, and then recites Iriyāvahiyaṁ, Tassa Uttari, and Annattha to frame the kāyotsarga.

segments.plainMeaning: Stand up, join both hands, hold the ritual hand-postures, and ask the guru's permission as described below.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: As shown earlier, having offered guru-vandana to the sthāpanājī (the installed image of the guru), rise to your feet, join both hands, and—maintaining the 'jina-mudrā' and the 'yoga-mudrā'—take the ādeśa (request for permission) as follows.
segments.explanation: A posture-setting direction: the devotee stands, joins the palms, and keeps jina-mudrā (heels slightly apart, body erect and still) and yoga-mudrā (fingers interlaced, palms hollowed like a lotus) before speaking the request. Nothing is chanted here.

ritualTerms.gloss: the installed symbolic representation of the guru/ācārya before whom the rite is done
the erect, still standing posture used in kāyotsarga
the hollowed, interlaced-finger hand-posture of veneration
the formal request for the guru's permission

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks: 'O reverend one, with your willing leave, may I make the confession for harm caused while moving?'
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Of your own gracious will, command me, O Lord (bhagavan)! Shall I perform the Iriyāvahiyaṁ paḍikkamaṇa—the turning-back and confession of harm done to living beings as I walked and moved about?
segments.explanation: The disciple (śiṣya) speaks this ādeśa as a question, seeking the guru's sanction before beginning. It leads into the Iriyāvahiyaṁ sūtra.

ritualTerms.gloss: 'by your own free will'—the courteous formula opening a request

segments.idiomaticTranslation: The guru grants leave: 'Perform the paḍikkamaṇa—proceed with the confession.'
segments.explanation: The guru's (guru) one-word response that authorizes the disciple to proceed.

ritualTerms.gloss: the reflective confession the disciple is now permitted to make

segments.plainMeaning: The disciple answers: 'I accept (as you wish).'
segments.idiomaticTranslation: 'Icchaṁ'—'So I gladly will,' the disciple's assent accepting the guru's command.
segments.explanation: The disciple's fixed formula of glad acceptance, spoken just before beginning the recitation.

segments.plainMeaning: Now recite the Iriyāvahiyaṁ, Tassa Uttari, and Annattha Ūsasieṇaṁ passages.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Recite, in order, the 'Iriyāvahiyaṁ' (the confession for harm to living beings while moving), then 'Tassa Uttari' (the resolve to purify oneself further through kāyotsarga), and 'Annattha Ūsasieṇaṁ' (the permitted bodily exceptions that do not break the kāyotsarga).
segments.explanation: A 'now recite X' cue naming the three sūtras that together frame the kāyotsarga: Iriyāvahiyaṁ confesses the fault, Tassa Uttari states the purifying intent, and Annattha lists the allowances before the devotee stands in kāyotsarga.

ritualTerms.gloss: 'abandonment of the body'—standing motionless in meditative stillness

notes: Segment 02 spells the call 'paḍikkamāmi' and segment 03 the response 'paḍikkameha' per the verbatim native text; rendered faithfully. Iriyāvahiyaṁ, Tassa Uttari, and Annattha placements follow the Devasi recitation tree (§2, §3.1, §4). The mudrā descriptions (jina-mudrā/yoga-mudrā postures) are standard tradition glosses and are not detailed in the supplied native segment; treated as conventional. No external sources web-verified for this step.
